trajectory_summary: The call moves from neutral to engaged on the back of Priyank's own disclosure energy — Randy's Google Maps personalization helped, but the customer did most of the work. Randy completed the verify section cleanly and booked a date, but left the appointment structurally fragile: no phone-vs-in-person clarification, no bill-submission urgency before the vacation, confirmation gated on bill receipt, and the highest-value bill-pain signal (EV + $350-400 summer bills) never flipped into emotional alignment. The call has a ceiling it never reached.
outcome_dispute: v1 tag is 'unknown.' The call ends with a tentative appointment booked for the 27th at 2PM, contingent on bill receipt. Outcome should be tagged 'conditional_booking' — not a clean sit, not a no-appointment. The two-week gap plus vacation plus bill-receipt gate make this appointment structurally vulnerable.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Okay. Gotcha. Alright. Perfect. I'll go ahead and put that down here.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Priyank just handed Randy the call's best leverage moment — EV load, $350 average bill, $400+ summer spikes — and Randy filed it as a data point. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, capture alone is not sufficient; the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. This is the single move that would have deepened Priyank's commitment before the two-week gap. Manager should walk Randy through the flip framework using Priyank's exact numbers from this call.

key_moments.quote: I got it pulled up on Google Maps, and, it definitely looks like a really nice area. Beautiful home, by the way, and it looks like, you get perfect sun exposure and plenty of roof space there.
key_moments.stewart_read: Randy's Google Maps personalization is the call's best rapport move. Naming the home, the sun exposure, and the roof space converts a cold verify beat into a moment of genuine specificity. This is the kind of micro-trade that reduces scam anxiety on a first call — 'I can see your house' is more credible than 'I'm from a solar company.' Worth reinforcing explicitly in the 1-on-1 as a technique Randy should repeat.

key_moments.quote: I also charge my EV card there, but it I had partially charged it. It means my work place also has the charges, so I don't charge it completely at home. I whenever I get a chance, I charge it. Like, let's say if I'm charging 600 kilowatt hours, I charge, like, 300 at home and 300 at at my work list. So you can have the $50.50. But $3.50 is a good estimate.
key_moments.stewart_read: Priyank has just disclosed an EV that adds meaningful load to his home bill — and he's already doing the math himself, estimating $350 as a reliable average with summer spikes above $400. This is the highest-value bill-pain signal in the call. A bill-pain flip is within setter scope (it's emotional alignment, not technical content — the scope-discipline deferral hypothesis doesn't hold here). Randy's response at 04:17 is purely administrative: 'Okay. Gotcha. Alright. Perfect. I'll go ahead and put that down here.' The flip was available and unused. Suggested intent (not verbatim): something that names the EV load as a growing cost and frames the appointment as the moment Priyank finds out what that number looks like with solar.

Randy captures the spouse-on-title disclosure at 02:37 and acknowledges it ('Okay. Sounds good.') but never returns to it. The appointment is booked without any attempt to include the wife or confirm she will be available on the 27th at 2PM. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct move is to include the spouse in the appointment — not overcome the objection, but make the appointment the venue for both decision-makers. Randy's handling is a protocol omission, not a strategic deferral.

key_moments.quote: I prefer afternoon, but maybe we just have to schedule something for two weeks from now. The reason being, this week is crazy for me. The following week, I'm going out for vacation. So and coming back on Friday. So it has to go from week of two weeks from now, like, 27th Avenue, anything after that.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the intervention point Randy missed. Priyank has just disclosed a two-week gap with a vacation in the middle — the exact conditions that produce a cooling-off no-show. The bill submission is already gated ('once I get that bill from you, I'll send you a confirmation'). Randy had a clean opening here to create pre-vacation urgency: something like 'Before things get crazy this week, can you grab that bill and send it over? That way I can lock in your confirmation before you head out.' Instead Randy accepts the timeline without comment and moves to day selection. The urgency moment passed unused.
